content.problemStatement:
Consider a string SSS consisting of lowercase Latin alphabets.

In one operation, you can pick an index from string SSS and replace the character at that index with 333 occurrences of the same character.

For example, if S=dabcS = \texttt{dabc}S=dabc, in one operation, we can choose the index 222 and replace S2S_2S2​ with 333 occurrences of S2S_2S2​. Thus, the string becomes S=daaa‾bcS =\texttt{d} \underline{\texttt{aaa}} \texttt{bc}S=daaa​bc.

Chefina has a string UUU of length NNN.

Help Chef design a string SSS of **minimum** length such that Chef can convert it to UUU using any (possibly zero) number of operations.

It is guaranteed that **exactly** one such SSS exists.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case consists of an integer NNN — the length of the string UUU.
The next line consists of string UUU.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the string SSS of **minimum** length, such that Chef can convert it to UUU using any (possibly zero) number of operations.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
U consists only of lowercase English letters
For every test case a unique minimal S exists (hence each run length in U is a power of 3).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N – the length of string U. The second line contains the string U consisting of N lowercase Latin letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the string S of minimum possible length such that U can be obtained from S by repeatedly replacing a character with three copies of the same character.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Each test case is reduced to the sequence of distinct consecutive characters: aaabbb → a|b → "ab"; abbbbbc → a|b|c → "abc"; abcd already has all characters distinct, so the answer is "abcd".

solution.approach:
The operation only multiplies the length of a single character block by 3. Therefore a character that appears consecutively in the original string S can only generate a block of that same character whose length is a power of 3 (1, 3, 9, 27, ...). Consequently, in the final string U each maximal block of equal characters must have a length that is a power of 3. The minimal possible S is obtained by taking exactly one character from each such block – i.e., the sequence of distinct consecutive characters of U. The problem guarantees that every block length is indeed a power of 3, so this construction is always valid and unique.
Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N and U (N is not needed for the algorithm).
2. Initialise an empty result string R.
3. Iterate over U; whenever the current character differs from the previous one (or i == 0) append it to R.
4. Output R.
The algorithm runs in O(N) time and O(1) additional memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of U. Over all test cases O(total N) ≤ 10^6.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input string and the output string.
